title: Resizing and Normalization
concept: Resizing shapes all images into a uniform grid, while normalization scales pixel values to small, centered numbers. These two preprocessing steps are essential for training stability and mathematical consistency.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is preprocessing applied to validation and test data, but augmentation is not?

options.text: Preprocessing makes images compatible; validation/testing needs consistent, unmodified images to measure performance fairly
Augmentation is too slow to run during validation
Preprocessing automatically labels the validation data

exercises.explain: Yes! Validation needs clean, consistent inputs to provide a fair score.

cards.title: Large Numbers, Slow Learning
cards.body: Image pixels are stored as integers from 0 to 255. If you pass these raw values directly into a deep network, the large numbers will cause activations to explode, leading to unstable training and vanishing/exploding gradients. We need to scale these values down.

cards.title: Resizing & Normalization
cards.body:
Resizing scales the width and height of all images to match the network's input grid (e.g., 224x224). Normalization scales pixel values. Typically, we first rescale pixels to 0.0-1.0, then subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation:
Normalized = (Pixel - Mean) / StdDev.

cards.title: Grading on a curve
cards.body: If one teacher grades tests out of 100 and another out of 10, comparing raw scores is confusing. Normalization is like converting all grades to a standard Z-score (centered at 0, with a spread of 1). It puts all pixel features on a level playing field.

cards.title: ImageNet Statistics
cards.body:
Most CNN backbones use standard pre-calculated statistics from the ImageNet dataset:
Mean: [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]
StdDev: [0.229, 0.224, 0.225] for RGB channels. Normalizing with these numbers aligns your data with what pretrained models expect.

cards.title: Don't just divide by 255
cards.body: While scaling pixels to 0-1 (by dividing by 255) is a good start, full normalization (subtracting mean, dividing by standard deviation) is better. Centering the data around 0 helps the optimizer converge much faster by stabilizing gradient updates.

cards.title: Raw vs. Normalized Pixels
cards.body: How pixel distributions change during normalization:

left.text:
Range: [0, 255] integers
Positives only
Uncentered; causes gradient instability

right.text:
Range: centered around 0.0 floats
Positive and negative values
Stable, balanced inputs for learning

exercises.prompt: What is the standard formula for pixel-level normalization?
exercises.explain: Correct! Normalization subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Normalizing pixel values to have a mean of 0 helps the training optimization process converge faster.
exercises.explain: True. Centering activations stabilizes gradient updates, leading to faster training convergence.

exercises.sentence: Resizing ensures all images match the exact first-layer input ___ expected by the CNN.
exercises.explain: Resizing reshapes width and height to match input layer dimensions (like 224x224).

exercises.prompt: Why should you use the ImageNet mean and standard deviation when using a pretrained backbone like ResNet?

options.text: To align your images with the exact numerical distribution the model was originally trained on
To automatically remove the background from your images
To speed up the network's forward pass by skipping activation layers

exercises.explain: Correct! Pretrained weights expect the same color and brightness distribution they learned from during pretraining.

exercises.prompt: You decide to skip normalization and pass raw [0, 255] pixels into a deep CNN. What is the most likely consequence?

options.text: Activations will explode in early layers, causing gradients to destabilize and preventing the model from training effectively
The model will train normally but will only be able to detect black and white shapes
The model will automatically compress itself to 1% of its original size
Nothing; normalization is optional and has no mathematical effect on training

exercises.explain: Exactly! Exploding activations wreck gradient updates. Next: why order of operations in the pipeline matters!
